name: tomato legume curry
instructions: shared base: bloom whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom) in fat, add red onions, then garlic + ginger, then ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, amchur). add tomato (form depends on variant — see notes). add legume + cooking liquid, simmer to consistency. finish with kasoori methi (and cilantro for the chickpea/lentil variants).
notes:
A FAMILY OF THREE: this recipe is the shared engine behind chana masala (chickpea), a lentil curry, and rajma (kidney bean). the spice base is identical; the variants differ in onion treatment, tomato form, peppers, fat, and finish.

THE CONSISTENCY AXIS: chickpeas want a stew, kidney beans want a gravy, lentils sit in between.

CHICKPEA VARIANT (stew end):
- chickpeas as the legume
- onions: chunky, not pureed
- tomato form: fresh tomatoes only (and/or canned tomatoes), NO tomato paste
- peppers: variety welcome — anaheim, serrano, even bell pepper
- brightness: lemon AND lemon zest (zest > juice)
- color/tannin: unflavored black tea (this is the chana signature)
- finish: cilantro is welcome, kasoori methi too

LENTIL VARIANT (middle):
- lentils as the legume
- onions: chunky
- tomato form: fresh tomatoes, tolerates some tomato paste
- peppers: minimal — not the lemon-and-peppers parade of the chickpea variant
- finish: BOTH cilantro and kasoori methi

KIDNEY BEAN VARIANT — RAJMA (gravy end):
- red beans as the legume
- onions: pureed (not chunky)
- tomato form: tomato paste, as concentrated as possible
- follows mexican logic — minimize anything that distracts from the glutamates and the kidney beans themselves
- fat: GHEE is non-optional here
- finish: kasoori methi over cilantro (rajma prefers methi)
- chili powder gets a bigger role here than in the chickpea version

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: should be in all three variants. it does color and bright heat without overpowering — different from generic chili powder.

KASOORI METHI: non-optional in rajma. welcome in all three variants. the dried fenugreek leaf is a load-bearing flavor, not a garnish.

SEE ALSO: there's an inverted version of the chickpea variant in this cookbook (inverted chana masala) — fries cooked rice in the spices/onions and adds boiled chickpeas at the end. more pilaf than curry. uses the chickpea-variant spice profile minus the black tea / fresh tomatoes.
